item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Might be Silence Suzuka, in which case the anomaly was later remotely contained by conversion into an anime girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is the whole point. It's a jab at people who point out the SCP is "a prison, not a hotel" in regards to how some of the accomodations are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending to this article legitimately made me burst out laughing. The world might fucking end because a horse. The things these poor Foundation folks have to deal with sometimes…
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I noticed a couple minor spelling mistakes throughout (could be UK English spellings?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that thing is turning around, you boys better get ready for a K-class. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe, but I doubt that Friendship is Anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm sure it has a lot to teach us about friendship…

item.title: Muh horsey!
item.content:encoded:
Quite well written and got a chuckle outta me. +1

Also, SCP:NOTAHOTEL should get its own upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP, but at the end, I'm sorry, I had to lol. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My problem with this is that we can't know that it is 10 light-years away, since a light year is the distance light can travel in a year. We only know that it was 10 light-years away ten years ago, and if it "appears to be turning," then that most likely means it has already turned and is heading back at us already, because if if we see it 10 light-years away, we are seeing it 10 years in the past. The observations of it that far away just don't make sense unless you do what was suggested and include that it is being observed through anomalous means. Altogether, however, this is a solid article, and I gave it +1.

item.title: The dangers of poor containment
item.content:encoded: I love that this entire article is dedicated to pointing out why supposedly comfy containment is needed. If it has been working, don't try to fix it. The foundation is not a hotel, but it's cold not cruel. If a little comfort is okay, then fine.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, if you pay close attention to the containment procedures, you'll see that the idea was to let the horse and its muscles atrophy until it would be unable to cause problems for the Foundation. Their theory was that, after fiveyears, they'd either have a horse that was perpetually freaking the [EXPLETIVE] out, or else one that got over it and was a more-or-less non-anomalous horse. Either way, it would be something the Foundation could handle, without spending too much resources. What they were not expecting was that the horse was capable of FTL space travel. And now we've got an XK-class end-of-world (galaxy?) scenario on our hands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In 201█, five years after SCP-2872 won the Kentucky Derby, it began to show signs of agitation, as it was unable to run or walk in its cell.

That seems like rather poor animal handling practices on the part of the Foundation.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I also believe it's a reference to Foundation policy; it refers to the often said critique meme, "The Foundation is a prison, not a hotel".

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: I believe it's a parody of Wikipedia's internal pages on policy. Editors there will reference policy using any number of shortcuts of that style, such as WP:NOTHERE (User is not here to build an encyclopedia), WP:CRYSTAL (Wikipedia is not a crystal ball), WP:TOOSOON (Article created too soon/user applies for adminship too soon), etc.

It's funny, and the reasons for the breach make a lot of sense, but there is one minor thing:

appeasement of SCP-2872, rather than actual protection from its manifestations - see SCP:NOTAHOTEL.

Is that deliberate, or was it supposed to be blackboxed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since the Kentucky Derby is an annual thing and the year is escape is in the 2010s, I'm gonna assume the horse left for space on 2010 and the time the SCP was last written was July 2015, the date this SCP was publicly released in this website. This means that the horse has been in space for at least five years, traveling at least ten light years. From this, I'm gonna assume the speed of the horse is almost twice the speed of light.

If this gets Freudian's blessing, the Earth is fucked.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"entered a trajectory towards the constellation Equuleus." ayy lmao

Hopefully it doesn't end up speeding up to a significant fraction of light speed, or else this horse will be the death of everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Its a horse
It goes fast
Nothing else to say really
Apart from I love it

item.title: Re: spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded: One line about "SCP-2872 remains observable through use of SCP-███" would totally work for me. It's a sufficient level of handwave. Because that's been used in space articles that I like before, and I can headcanon them as being the same skip.

item.title: Re: spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
how can they tell how far away it is? A horse is tiny compared to literally everything else in space

'cause my big horsy is also magic, that's why! <G>

item.title: spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

My only real question is, how can they tell how far away it is? A horse is tiny compared to literally everything else in space, and if it's somehow achieved faster-than-light travel, it would be doubly invisible. Like a meta-invisible.

However, this is hilarious, and I can't downvote comedy that made me laugh in good faith.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted with four hooves and a swish of the tail!

This is very good.

So, is it Secretariat, Broad Appeal or Calidoscopio? <G>
(videos of those races on request)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like owner, like horse…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This conversation was fairly resolved, I thought.

I personally can't tell just by looking that Vin Diesel is African American, and that some random Bollywood star with really dark skin is actually both Caucasian and South Asian. Maybe it's just me.

The darkest skinned person I ever met was Caucasian…

Also in this case it seemed to me like the "Magical Negro" cliche, maybe it wasn't. I don't know. I am not really sure I care.

Wasn't there a spin-off thread for this?

I don't understand why you have this issue only for this specific SCP

Why would you ever say that? I think I have been fairly consistent about this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand why you have this issue only for this specific SCP. For every single humanoid SCP on the site, it's standard policy to give a physical description. SCP's that look Asian are described to look Asian, just like how SCPs who look female are described as such. We don't care about what the guy "identifies" as because that is not a visual marker we can tell him by. I mean, there's a chance that the guy actually identifies as a woman—not gonna stop the Foundation from logging down his physical appearance.

Just because something is subjective, doesn't make it not useful information.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MUST UPVOTE SPACE HORSE

Current analysis suggests that it is turning.

THIS IS THE BEST PART

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Similarly, we can assume that since its being mentioned this guy is very distinctly of African heritage

Again, though how can you tell? Merely being dark skinned isn't enough. I suppose he could have been dressed and talked like B.B. King or something which would at least be a sufficient cultural identifier.

I am still not sure that I really feel all that passionately about it, other than it being of great social significance in general.

My issue is that isn't relevant here at all. Again why, in a clinical document, do we care what some anonymous agent guessed this guy identifies as? How is that not purely subjective?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually the Rock thing kind of goes along with what I was saying: It might not be useful to describe someone who is a little bit taller than average as tall, but describing someone over 2 m as tall is a very good distinguishing characteristic. Similarly, we can assume that since its being mentioned this guy is very distinctly of African heritage, as opposed to a person of more eclectic, ambiguous ancestry, like Mr. Johnson.

Also added a new post to the prior discussion in the forums, regarding the artistic take on terms like "African" "Asian" or "Caucasian" as terms for general physical descriptions- there are pretty well established definitions in that context.

I've noticed you do seem to feel very strongly about the subject of ancestry and racial identity. Keep in mind, personal feelings on a subject can lead to reading more into something than is really there. (My dad's second generation Irish and he grew up in Atlantic City in the 60's and 70's. Has the scars to prove it. I get it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have a problem with that too, especially since nobody seems to have a clue what that actually means.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We totally specify that people are caucasian even when it has no bearing on anything. I agree that the Magical Negro trope can be problematic, but I don't think it applies here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On his mother's side, on his father's side he's Afro-Canadian. I am certain that I read an interview where he identitifed as black.

But if not him how about Vin Diesel?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Rock is Samoan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it may have been better if he were said to be black and that alone

I'd prefer "appeared to be a middle age man". Why do we care what some agent guessed what race this guy identifies as? In this setting why are we assuming he even identifies as Homo sapien?

But come on, sirpudding, you can tell where someone is from after having spoken to them. Someone who lives in Sri Lanka, America, or Australia all their lives would have speech habits the Foundation would easily pick up on.

Accents aren't genetic. Southeast Asian Americans or Australian Aborigine- Americans aren't going to sound different from anybody else from here for the same reason I wasn't born speaking Gealic.

Someone who is dark skinned and lived in the US all their lives could be from Southeast Asia or Australia and wouldn't be "African American" except in the sense that we are all from Africa. I work with a guy who is from Sri Lanka, has lived in the US since he has a teenager, talks like all the other sixty year old dudes at my work and is darker than most of my black friends (one of whom is like 400% more WASP than I am).

You can't tell racial identity from how someone looks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it may have been better if he were said to be black and that alone. But come on, sirpudding, you can tell where someone is from after having spoken to them. Someone who lives in Sri Lanka, America, or Australia all their lives would have speech habits the Foundation would easily pick up on.

I still really don't see this as a magical negro stereotype at all, but I can see where you'd see that. I do agree the Foundation certainly would, uh, strive to probably preserve whiteness through its weird conservative undertones, but I think it's just so incidental to the scip that it's worthless to call the previous owner a magical negro. If that makes sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
happens to be african-american

"Happens to be black" seems extremely problematic to me. How come nobody ever "happens to be white"?

which is one of the few things we know about him

How? There's plenty of dark skinned people who aren't African American and plenty of African Americans who look like "white people" (did you know that Dwayne "The Rock" Johnson is a black man?). Did he fill out a form with some of those race bubbles before he vanished? Racial identity is totally a social construct, there's no way to tell it without asking someone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
comes from this black figure of old time wisdom teaching and doing magic for a white person and a mostly white audience.

He shows up, gives a magic horse to the Foundation (which if not "the man" I don't know what is; it even keeps a mostly non-white slave labor force) and disappears. Seems like a magical negro to me…

I've seen it used in a lot of works that specifically refer to a scip or someone affected as Caucasian.

Yeah, I think "Caucasian" is basically meaningless and I'd rather see it never used except to literally mean people from the Caucuses. Anthropologically it includes practically everybody who isn't East Asian or Sub-Saharan African. As a racial identify it excludes even the people for whom it is named…

I'd personally rather just not see racial identity unless it is actually relevant. How do they even know what this guy identifies as? Did they have him fill out a form before he vanished? How do you know he isn't from Sri Lanka? Or Australia?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wouldn't call this the magical old black guy trope. More like "the rich guy who gave the field agents a fake name and contact information after happily dumping his probably anomalously-enhanced horse off on the foundation and promptly fleeing the country to avoid having to deal with $30 million dollars in damages including the destruction of a three-mile tract of I-24, who happens to be african-american, which is one of the few things we know about him."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I first read this, that was the first conclusion I jumped to. But, I think it's difference, because normally the stereotype, and the offensiveness, comes from this black figure of old time wisdom teaching and doing magic for a white person and a mostly white audience. There's this kind of tokenization of the figure of the black male as, like, this magic old "uncle" stereotype.

Whereas, this is just a fast dude with a fast horse.

But, personally, I do think the Foundation would note the general appearance of this person. I've seen it used in a lot of works that specifically refer to a scip or someone affected as Caucasian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last line is really good, but the rest of this just doesn't hold together. There's an awful lot of physics-breaking (and not just by the horse), and plenty of stupidity on the parts of all parties involved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
unknown African-American male in his late 50s,

Magical old black guy stereotype? Race is relevant, why?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course the horse is turning back towards Earth. The Kentucky Derby ends where it begins, doesn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was on the fence until the bit about possibly turning…heading back towards earth? Brace for impact. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would watch the heck of a show with this as opening.

As for the article, its simple, its interesting, it has horses and space. Yep, thats an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's just a fast guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the owner moving that fast would warrant him being classified as SCP-2872-1, or at the very least a POI.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. Sorry for earth that it's not a quarter-horse. My only complaint is echoed above. Perhaps the prior owner had a heart attack when we captured him? He physically 'disappeared' in front of witnesses? Faded out of existence while being restrained?

+1 regardless, though I'd like to see that addressed in some manner. Awesome concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I should probably make it clearer that he left at the same speed as the horse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
left before he could be questioned further.

No one tried to stop him? This really makes me want to downvote because it makes the Foundation seem incompetent

Current analysis suggests that it is turning.

I withdraw my downvote.

Right now it's a no vote for me. If the part about the guy just leaving before he could be questioned is changed to make more sense, it would be an easy upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NEW TO SATURDAY MORNINGS: IT'S SPACE HORSE
space hoooorse he's up in space
space hoooooorse his relativistic speeds will cause significant problems for the human race
space horse he'll go fast, he'll never come in last
space horse knows the problems he must face but
space horse he will win the race
SPACE HORSE!

Thank you kinchtheknifeblade for being a guiding light of inspiration for this SCP about a horse who just wants to show everyone he can win the Kentucky Derby.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm this horse. I'm the spacehorse. I will never feel pain in the darkness of space.

But honestly, I enjoy this, because I see this piece as being both kind of "the foundation gets too stiff and fucks up" but also it's kind of sweet. Because this is a good horse. We all know. We can tell. +1
